items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: Libet's 1985 study suggested brain activity precedes conscious decision by roughly how many milliseconds?
items.why: It's 300ms. “100ms” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Heritability of IQ in modern twin studies is approximately what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 0.5. “0.1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many gene-environment relationships did Plomin propose?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
